skill.name: istio-traffic-management
skill.description: Configure Istio traffic management for routing, load balancing, circuit breakers, retries, mirroring, gateways, and canary releases.
skill.summary: Plan and review Istio traffic policies for service mesh routing and resilience.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many markdown code fences as Ruby backtick execution and weak cryptography, but the inspected file contains documentation and YAML examples rather than executable skill code. The main residual risk is operational: applying the Istio examples or debug commands without review can change live routing, gateway exposure, mirroring, and fault-injection behavior.

medium_findings.title: Operational Traffic Policy Risk
medium_findings.description: The skill provides Istio examples for canary routing, circuit breaking, traffic mirroring, fault injection, and ingress gateways. These are legitimate DevOps patterns, but applying them directly can alter production traffic, expose hosts, mirror requests, or inject failures.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly contains traffic-management templates that affect routing and gateway behavior. The risk is operational misuse, not malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Cluster Inspection Commands Documented
medium_findings.description: The debugging section lists istioctl commands that inspect routes, endpoints, and logging configuration in a cluster. These commands are normal for Istio troubleshooting, but they should be run only against intended clusters and with appropriate access controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented as examples and are not automatically executed. They can still reveal service topology or alter proxy log levels when used by an operator.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings correspond to markdown fenced code blocks and diagrams. The file does not contain Ruby code or instructions that automatically execute these fenced blocks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line inspection shows markdown code fences containing YAML, a simple diagram, and bash examples. No executable Ruby backtick expression is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm detections match ordinary Istio terms, YAML fields, or documentation text. No hashing, encryption, credential generation, or cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain metadata, Istio resource names, route destinations, TLS mode examples, and documentation links. They do not implement or recommend a weak cryptographic algorithm.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Are Benign Network References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to official Istio documentation. They do not receive data from the skill and do not indicate exfiltration or unauthorized network behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are static links to istio.io reference pages. No request code, credential handling, or data transfer logic is present.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Is Low Context
low_findings.description: The cited line is part of a consistent hashing example that references an HTTP header and optional source IP behavior. It is configuration guidance, not a reconnaissance routine.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line appears in a load-balancing configuration example. No host probing, network scanning, or system inventory logic was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Potentially Disruptive Istio Configuration Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include traffic mirroring at full percentage, fault injection, gateway exposure, and canary traffic weights. These patterns are expected for the skill but require environment-specific review before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are directly visible and can affect service traffic if applied. They are legitimate patterns with deployment risk.

content.value_statement: Istio traffic management can be difficult to configure safely across routing, retries, gateways, and progressive delivery. This skill provides practical templates and review guidance for service mesh traffic policies.
actual_capabilities: Explains core Istio traffic resources, including VirtualService, DestinationRule, Gateway, and ServiceEntry.
Provides YAML patterns for basic routing, canary releases, retries, timeouts, and circuit breakers.
Shows examples for traffic mirroring, fault injection, ingress gateways, and load balancing.
Lists debugging commands for analyzing routes, endpoints, and proxy log configuration.
Summarizes practical best practices for retries, timeouts, subsets, monitoring, and rollout strategy.
limitations: It does not validate manifests against a live Kubernetes cluster.
It does not replace environment-specific security, capacity, or compliance review.
It does not include automation scripts or deployment tooling.
Examples use placeholder service names and must be adapted before use.

use_cases.title: Plan a Canary Release
use_cases.description: Create a weighted routing approach that sends a small percentage of traffic to a new service version.

use_cases.title: Improve Service Resilience
use_cases.description: Add timeouts, retries, connection pool settings, and outlier detection to reduce cascading failures.

use_cases.title: Review Mesh Routing Changes
use_cases.description: Check proposed VirtualService and DestinationRule changes before they are applied to shared environments.

prompt_templates.title: Explain Basic Routing
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how to route traffic between two Istio service subsets for my service. Keep the explanation beginner friendly.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user is learning how VirtualService and DestinationRule work together.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Canary Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Istio canary traffic plan for service SERVICE_NAME with stable and canary subsets. Include rollout verification steps.
prompt_templates.scenario: A team wants a controlled release path for a new version.

prompt_templates.title: Review Resilience Settings
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these intended Istio resilience settings for timeouts, retries, circuit breaking, and outlier detection. Identify risky defaults.
prompt_templates.scenario: An SRE wants feedback before applying production traffic policy changes.

prompt_templates.title: Design Advanced Traffic Testing
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an Istio traffic mirroring and fault-injection strategy for staging. Include safety limits, rollback checks, and observability needs.
prompt_templates.scenario: An advanced user needs a controlled test plan for service mesh behavior.

output_examples.input: I need a safe canary rollout for my payments service.
output_examples.output: A recommended response would define stable and canary subsets, start with a low canary weight, add route validation, and include rollback criteria.

output_examples.input: My service has intermittent downstream failures.
output_examples.output: A recommended response would suggest explicit timeouts, bounded retries, connection pool limits, outlier detection, and monitoring checks.

output_examples.input: I want to test behavior under latency and errors.
output_examples.output: A recommended response would propose staging-only fault injection, small percentages, clear success metrics, and a rollback plan.

best_practices: Start with low-risk routing changes and increase traffic only after metrics are healthy.
Set explicit timeouts, retry limits, and circuit breaker thresholds for critical services.
Validate generated policy names, hosts, subsets, namespaces, and gateways before applying manifests.
anti_patterns: Do not apply placeholder YAML directly to production clusters.
Do not mirror sensitive production traffic into untrusted or undersecured environments.
Do not combine high retry counts with long timeouts on overloaded services.

faq.question: Can this skill deploy Istio resources for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples, but it does not run deployment commands.

faq.question: Does this skill require access to my Kubernetes cluster?
faq.answer: No. The skill content is documentation. Any cluster commands must be run separately by an authorized operator.

faq.question: Can I use the examples as production manifests?
faq.answer: Use them as starting points only. Update service names, namespaces, hosts, subsets, and safety limits first.

faq.question: Does it cover canary and blue-green releases?
faq.answer: It covers weighted routing patterns that support canary releases and related progressive delivery workflows.

faq.question: Does it include troubleshooting guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It lists common istioctl commands for analyzing configuration, routes, endpoints, and proxy logging.

faq.question: What should I review before using fault injection?
faq.answer: Review the target environment, percentage values, error types, observability, and rollback process before enabling it.
